title: Hierarchical Bits: A Structural Envelope for Orchestrating Representations — Method, Measurements and Boundaries
description:
Hierarchical Bits (BH) is a data-format paradigm investigated as a structural envelope rather than a codec. This technical note tests the hypothesis ("the byte is an implicit tree; hierarchy is interpretation") across nine independent angles — image codec, database aggregation, Merkle verification, co-registered layers (wafer/video), GPU data movement, multimodal AI storage, symbolic/compositional density, decode-as-program, and orchestration — each with declared falsifiable claims, exact correctness as a gate, and honest baselines distinguishing gains vs naive from gains vs state of the art.

Central finding: BH does not compress dense signal better than JPEG/WebP/AVIF (it loses — measured), but as a structural envelope it (a) makes structure explicit at 0–6% cost, (b) routes each region's residual to the right specialist, and (c) offers multiple reads over one structure. Anchor result: a structured document is 2.1× smaller than WebP and readable per-region in 3–55× fewer bytes, within one file. A usable prototype (bhmem, agent memory) accompanies the study. The transversal law: BH pays off to the extent the data is structure, not signal; the boundary is structure recognition, not entropy.
